claim: On November 21, 2019, Israeli Attorney General Avichai Mandelblit formally indicted sitting Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on three counts of corruption — bribery, fraud, and breach of trust — making him the first sitting Israeli Prime Minister to be criminally charged. The indictments stem from three separate investigations: Case 1000 (the “Gifts Affair,” alleging that Netanyahu and his family accepted approximately $230,000 in luxury gifts from billionaire Arnon Milchan and others over several years in exchange for political favors); Case 2000 (alleging a 2008-2014 quid-pro-quo arrangement with Yedioth Ahronoth publisher Arnon Mozes for favorable coverage in exchange for legislation limiting a rival newspaper); and Case 4000 (the most serious, alleging that Netanyahu granted regulatory favors worth approximately $500 million to the Israeli telecom Bezeq in exchange for favorable coverage on the Bezeq-owned news site Walla! — the case that carries a bribery count with a maximum ten-year sentence). The trial opened on May 24, 2020 and has continued through 2026; Netanyahu's own cross-examination began in June 2025. The Israeli judicial-overhaul package his coalition pursued in 2023, which would have limited the Supreme Court's power to review government conduct and the judicial-appointment process, was the political backdrop of the trial.

sources.label: Wikipedia — “Trial of Benjamin Netanyahu,” canonical aggregator with case-by-case index and primary-source citations.
Al Jazeera — “Benjamin Netanyahu's corruption trial, what you need to know” (December 9, 2024).
Middle East Eye — “Netanyahu corruption trials: What are the charges and will he be pardoned?”
The Jerusalem Post — running coverage, including “Case 4000 cross-examination ends, prosecution advances to Case 2000.”

notes: The trial is ongoing as of mid-2026. Connects to the existing 2023-israel-judicial-protests-001 record: the judicial-overhaul package Netanyahu's coalition pursued in 2023 would have given the governing coalition expanded authority over judicial appointments and constrained the Supreme Court's ability to review government conduct — including, by direct implication, the prosecution then proceeding against the Prime Minister.
